title: How to Travel INDIA on $30 a Day | Budget Travel Tips
text: This is incredible. Tiruvannamalai, Tamil Nadu state, South India. Hey guys, how's it going? Today is Wednesday, March 18th, 2026. Currently 91° F out there. That is 33° C. So, one of the first videos I made that did fairly well was a video about how to travel in India on $10 a day, which I filmed in Rishikesh. And so, traveling in India on $10 a day these days is definitely not especially practical. I wouldn't say that it is impossible, but almost certainly, especially if you are traveling around and not just staying in one place longer term, then most likely you're going to spend more than that. I would say a uh reasonable budget for India is 25 to $50 US per day. Pardon the construction noise out there. So, in this video, I thought that I would make an updated version and show you how to travel India on $30 per day. Now, I've already gotten pretty close to that as it is, but what I have purchased so far covers the three basics, which are one, accommodation, number two, food. Showed my breakfast there. And then number three, transportation. I have a motorbike that is 400 rupees per day. That is about $4. So, the room here is 1,300 rupees. That is $14. So, the exchange rate for the rupee versus the US dollar is amazing now. It's 92 rupees per US dollar. So, almost 100 to one. And so, $14 for the room, breakfast was like $5.50, and then the bike there, $4.25. And so, you add all that up and you get around $23 US. And so, I have just $7 now left to spend for the day, but I don't have any big plans. There is really nothing much else I plan to pay for other than dinner tonight. I generally have two meals per day, a late breakfast and a dinner at normal uh dinner hour. So, uh I am going to head out, hop on the motorbike there, the scooter, and go for a hike up on Mount Arunachala. I'm not going to try to hike up the mountain. That is apparently illegal to uh climb the mountain, probably because it is a holy mountain here in uh Tiruvannamalai. Many uh people come here, both Indians and foreigners, for various reasons, but because of its spiritual significance. You will be seeing the uh mountain soon. In fact, let's uh get out there and uh we'll give you the view. It's actually a cloudy and a bit cooler day than previous days, which is nice. But uh here you go, Mount Arunachala. And so, the main uh town of Tiruvannamalai is about a 10-minute or so drive out that way. There is a famous temple and the Ramana Maharshi Ashram. Now, uh one of the nice things about Tiruvannamalai is that there is so much to do around here. So, as I have shown in my previous videos, there are some great swimming spots. There are lots of events. I went to a pool party with my friend Adam, who I will be trying to meet up with in a little while. And then, all kinds of musical singing, whether spiritual type events or just gatherings. At the pool party, they were playing hip-hop music, so there's a lot to do in this area. Now, on the subject of uh uh expenses, here's one, laundry. And so, I actually kind of showed my laundry strategy. Get back down to the room. Which is that I mostly do it myself. If there are self-service laundromats available somewhere that I will quite possibly take advantage of that. But uh basically how I tend to do it is do a few items at a time rather than having a uh big pile of laundry that takes you, you know, a while to uh tackle. I will often just do the laundry in the shower. You already have lots of water and soap coming down and then just throw it on the ground there. And so, I just uh washed a couple of things there today. And so, laundry for free. All right. So, my friend Adam is a little ahead of me. He is already hiking on the mountain at this moment. And so, I'm going to get out of here and go see if I happen to find him. If I don't find him there, then I will hopefully end up finding him elsewhere today at some point. We shall see. So, here we go. Let's get out of here and uh go see a bit more of Tiruvannamalai. My time here is getting close to finished. Tomorrow is my last day and I then have a uh flight booked to elsewhere in India. You'll see where later. So, uh let's get cruising. A wildlife sighting. Looks like a turkey. All right. So, uh this This guest house. Sai guest house. Love my room there. And here is the bike. And I should mention this is actually kind of expensive for a motorbike here. I rented it from them. So it's called a Sai guest house because that there is Sai Baba. And so uh I rented it from the uh guest house here, but Adam has a bike for either 100 or 150 rupees per day in part because he's renting it longer term. Also, my bike is a little bit nicer, but uh anyways, you can rent a bike for like a dollar to $4-ish. Either way, that is a great price. So this is the main road that goes around the mountain. It is a loop, 14 km, about 9 miles, and it is one of the uh traditions of the people who come here is to walk all the way around. Looks like these guys are doing the walk. Hello. They're barefoot. But uh most people do it later in the evening. Maybe a few more doing it uh today because it is a little bit cooler. But anyways, I wanted to uh mention here one of the most obvious things about traveling if you want to save some money, which is to eat where the locals eat. So I showed my breakfast, a Western-style breakfast, a little expensive for India, $5.50. Still, you know, obviously very, very reasonable, but uh last night Adam and I ate right there. We both had a masala dosa, and they were 80 rupees. So uh less than a buck for dinner there. All right, now time for me to head that way and uh go start the hike up to the cave. So an ATM there. I got plenty of cash for now, but that is the main way that I get my local currency when I travel is using an ATM. It is just the most practical way to go. You will get the fairest exchange rate compared to actually having to change money and it is much more convenient to just stop and go to an ATM rather than having to deal with changing money, especially in certain places where you might have to go to a bank or who knows what. And you won't get as good of a rate. And also that way you don't have to carry as much cash on you. But I do recommend having an emergency stash of cash of a few hundred dollars. All right, so uh the main temple in the city here is right down there. The cave is up on the side of the mountain up there. Which was I turned off completely my phone. So, before going for meditations because I did in probably five spots today. Which in the Ramana Ashram first. Then in one cave, the second cave, and and also in two spots. In one spot you just saw me. >> Mhm. >> Uh so, that was also a little bit meditation. And uh because I turn off my phone completely which is for me it's good idea because I'm not tempted to take photos or check my messages. So, I could really focus like inwards. So, really on meditation and chill and I was completely in a different state. Beautiful state, I would say. And then when I uh turn off my phone, of course, it was like phone call and many messages. So, that would be at the hill or in the cave it would be natural distractions. I would like writing messages instead of being here and now. So, that's a suggestion just to if you want to chill, if you want to meditate, if you want to be in a like relaxing state, just turn off your phone. And be here and now and enjoy the weather, the nature, or wherever you are. >> It's a great tip for people in general.
